claims.claim_text: The opening personal Hubble debugging anecdote is presented as a real experience without attribution.
claims.anchor_text: We had a microservices architecture with enough moving parts that a network-policy mistake was hard to isolate. One service was failing health checks even though direct application tests passed, and each team initially believed its own layer was fine.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a microservices architecture with enough moving parts, a network-policy mistake can be hard to isolate. A service may fail health checks even when direct application tests pass, and each team may initially believe its own layer is fine.
claims.rationale: The paragraph reads as a real operational anecdote but has no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote says Hubble identified the policy drop three seconds later.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The relevant drop appeared quickly:
claims.rationale: The precise timing is unsupported and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: Cilium uses an eBPF-based dataplane and provides networking, observability, security, identity-aware policy, kube-proxy replacement, and Hubble visibility.
claims.rationale: The Cilium README directly describes its eBPF dataplane, identity-based L3-L7 policy model, kube-proxy replacement, and Hubble observability.

claims.claim_text: Every Kubernetes cluster runs kube-proxy.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many Kubernetes clusters run kube-proxy, although some use alternate Service implementations such as Cilium's kube-proxy replacement.
claims.rationale: The absolute claim is false for kube-proxy-free clusters.

claims.claim_text: In iptables mode, kube-proxy rules scale with Services and endpoints, and iptables has scaling problems for Service proxying in large clusters.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes nftables blog explains iptables-mode kube-proxy rule scaling, O(n) lookup, and large-cluster programming latency.

claims.claim_text: A 500-Service cluster produces exactly 12,847 iptables-save lines.
claims.suggested_rewrite: # Output: many thousands of lines
claims.rationale: The exact count is presented as factual output but is not sourced and will vary by cluster.

claims.claim_text: A 5,000-Service cluster produces exactly 147,291 iptables-save lines.
claims.suggested_rewrite: # Output: a very large ruleset
claims.rationale: The exact count is unsupported and environment-dependent.

claims.claim_text: The debugging sketch references exactly 147,000 iptables rules.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Somewhere in this large ruleset.
claims.rationale: The precise figure repeats an unsupported illustrative count.

claims.claim_text: The update section claims exactly 147,000 iptables rules are rewritten.
claims.suggested_rewrite: TIME TO UPDATE A LARGE IPTABLES RULESET
claims.rationale: The precise rule count is unsupported and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: Large-cluster kube-proxy iptables rewrites take about 5-30 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. Can become slow enough to affect Service update latency on large clusters
claims.rationale: The timing range is a benchmark-like claim without a specific benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: At scale, Service and endpoint changes happen dozens of times per minute.
claims.anchor_text: At scale: dozens of times per minute
claims.suggested_rewrite: At scale: repeatedly during normal workload churn
claims.rationale: The frequency is unsupported and should not be presented as a measured statistic.

claims.claim_text: Datadog and Shopify are named as examples of organizations that wrote about hitting kube-proxy/iptables limits.
claims.anchor_text: This isn't a hypothetical. [Datadog wrote about hitting this limit](https://www.datadoghq.com/blog/engineering/introducing-glommio/). So did [Shopify](https://shopify.engineering/resiliency-planning-how-we-prepared-for-black-friday). At larger cluster sizes, iptables-based service routing can become a real operational bottleneck.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At larger cluster sizes, iptables-based service routing can become a real operational bottleneck.
claims.rationale: The linked named-company evidence is off-allowlist and the Shopify link does not clearly support the stated kube-proxy limit.

claims.claim_text: Standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies are based on IP addresses.
claims.anchor_text: Standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies have a different problem: they're based on IP addresses.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicies describe L3/L4 allow rules with selectors and ports, but many data planes still translate those selectors into concrete forwarding or filtering state.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy is selector-based at the API level; the original sentence overstates the abstraction.

claims.claim_text: eBPF is extended BPF, supports dynamic insertion of bytecode into the Linux kernel, runs at kernel hook points, and is checked by an in-kernel verifier/JIT path.
claims.rationale: Cilium's component overview describes eBPF's packet-filter origin, extensions, verifier, JIT compiler, and kernel hook points.

claims.claim_text: Traditional packet processing costs roughly 50-100 microseconds per packet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cost: extra latency from additional processing steps
claims.rationale: The precise per-packet latency range is an uncited benchmark.

claims.claim_text: The eBPF packet path costs roughly 5-10 microseconds per packet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cost: lower latency by keeping many datapath decisions in kernel
claims.rationale: The precise per-packet latency range is an uncited benchmark.

claims.claim_text: The eBPF path is 10x faster with zero userspace involvement for most packets.
claims.anchor_text: 10x faster. Zero userspace involvement for most packets.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Fewer userspace transitions for many datapath decisions.
claims.rationale: The speedup and zero-userspace phrasing are unsupported benchmark-style claims.

claims.claim_text: The Linux eBPF verifier rejects unsafe programs by checking control flow, memory access, register state, helper-call constraints, and other safety properties.
claims.rationale: The kernel verifier documentation directly describes the verifier's safety checks and rejection behavior.

claims.claim_text: The eBPF verifier has a 1,000,000-instruction complexity limit.
claims.rationale: The Linux kernel header defines BPF_COMPLEXITY_LIMIT_INSNS as 1000000.

claims.claim_text: The Cilium team has contributed extensively to the Linux kernel verifier.
claims.anchor_text: The Cilium team has contributed extensively to the Linux kernel to make the verifier smarter while maintaining safety. Writing eBPF programs that pass the verifier is an art—Cilium handles this complexity so you don't have to.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The verifier can reject programs that seem safe to a human, and writing eBPF programs that pass those checks takes specialized care. Cilium handles much of this complexity so application teams do not have to.
claims.rationale: The contribution-history claim is specific but unsourced.

claims.claim_text: Cilium's architecture includes per-node agents, an operator, a CNI plugin, Hubble server, Hubble Relay, Hubble CLI, and Hubble UI roles.
claims.rationale: The component overview describes the Cilium agent, operator, CNI plugin, Hubble server, Relay, CLI, and GUI.

claims.claim_text: cilium connectivity test deploys test workloads and validates pod, service, external, DNS, policy, and Hubble flow behavior.
claims.rationale: The Cilium CLI README documents connectivity-test validation steps and Hubble flow validation.

claims.claim_text: Cilium identities are derived from labels, are cluster-wide unique numeric identifiers, can be shared by endpoints with the same security-relevant labels, and are used for policy enforcement.
claims.rationale: Cilium terminology docs directly define identity derivation, cluster-wide numeric identifiers, shared identities, and policy use.

claims.claim_text: Cilium reserves identity numbers below 256, with host=1, world=2, and health=4.
claims.rationale: Cilium's numeric identity code defines MinimalNumericIdentity as 256 and enumerates the reserved host, world, and health identities.

claims.claim_text: Cilium supports standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, CiliumNetworkPolicy with L3-L7 rules, and CiliumClusterwideNetworkPolicy for cluster-scoped policy.
claims.rationale: The Cilium Kubernetes policy docs list all three policy formats and their scope.

claims.claim_text: Cilium L7 HTTP rules can match methods, paths, and headers, and non-matching HTTP requests are denied at L7.
claims.rationale: The L7 policy docs define HTTP method/path/header matching and state that requests not matching rules are denied.

claims.claim_text: Cilium DNS/FQDN policy uses DNS responses and DNS proxy/cache behavior to allow IPs returned for matched FQDNs while respecting TTLs.
claims.rationale: The Layer 3 policy docs describe DNS-based rules, DNS proxy data collection, cached DNS responses, and TTL handling.

claims.claim_text: The module lists `dns` as a Cilium toEntities semantic entity.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | DNS endpoints | DNS servers such as kube-dns/CoreDNS, usually selected with `toEndpoints` or DNS policy rules |
claims.rationale: The Cilium entity list documents host, remote-node, kube-apiserver, health, world, and related entities, but not a `dns` entity.

claims.claim_text: Hubble provides distributed Cilium/eBPF-based flow visibility, service maps, metrics, DNS/HTTP/TCP flow details, and network policy observation.
claims.rationale: The Hubble README directly documents flow visibility, service dependency graph, metrics, DNS/HTTP/TCP examples, and network policy behavior.

claims.claim_text: Hubble can expose Prometheus metrics and can be enabled with metrics such as dns, drop, tcp, flow, icmp, and http.
claims.rationale: The Hubble setup docs cover enabling Hubble and troubleshooting metrics configuration.

claims.claim_text: Hubble captures flows with no sampling and provides a complete record of all network communication.
claims.anchor_text: Hubble captures flows using eBPF, which means there's no sampling. Compared with coarse network monitoring, Hubble gives detailed flow-level visibility that is especially useful for troubleshooting and auditing. This makes Hubble invaluable for security auditing—you have a complete record of all network communication.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Hubble captures detailed flow events from Cilium's eBPF-powered dataplane. Compared with coarse network monitoring, Hubble gives flow-level visibility that is especially useful for troubleshooting and auditing, subject to the configured flow buffer and retention limits.
claims.rationale: Hubble internals document a bounded ring buffer that overwrites older events, so 'complete record' is too strong.

claims.claim_text: Cilium can replace kube-proxy with eBPF and handle ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, and externalIP Services.
claims.rationale: The kube-proxy-free docs state that Cilium's eBPF kube-proxy replacement handles those Kubernetes Service types.

claims.claim_text: After Cilium kube-proxy replacement, iptables-save reports exactly 127 rules.
claims.suggested_rewrite: # much smaller, depending on host networking and Cilium configuration
claims.rationale: The exact post-migration iptables count is an unsupported environment-specific statistic.

claims.claim_text: The performance table is introduced as real production-cluster benchmarks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Common performance tradeoffs to evaluate in your own cluster:
claims.rationale: The table provides qualitative guidance but no benchmark source or measurement methodology.

claims.claim_text: Cilium's kube-proxy replacement supports Direct Server Return mode controlled by loadBalancer.mode=dsr, allowing backend replies to bypass the load balancer path.
claims.rationale: The kube-proxy-free docs describe DSR mode, the loadBalancer.mode Helm setting, and backend direct replies.

claims.claim_text: Cilium encrypts all pod-to-pod traffic with one flag.
claims.anchor_text: Encrypting all pod-to-pod traffic sounds hard. With Cilium, it's one flag.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Encrypting traffic between Cilium-managed pods on different nodes sounds hard. With Cilium, it is a configuration flag.
claims.rationale: Cilium's WireGuard docs distinguish which traffic is encrypted; same-node and other paths have caveats.

claims.claim_text: Cilium supports WireGuard transparent encryption via encryption.enabled=true and encryption.type=wireguard.
claims.rationale: The WireGuard transparent encryption docs show the same Cilium install options and explain node-to-node tunnel setup.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard transparent encryption adds about 5% overhead.
claims.suggested_rewrite: • Overhead depends on workload, CPU, MTU, and routing path
claims.rationale: The exact overhead figure is an uncited benchmark and varies by environment.

claims.claim_text: The Christmas policy story is framed as a war story with a specific seasonal event.
claims.anchor_text: ## War Story: The Policy That Ate Christmas
claims.suggested_rewrite: ## Failure Mode: A Policy Blocks an Overlooked Dependency
claims.rationale: The section is a fabricated/composite anecdote and should not be framed as an attributable war story.

claims.claim_text: The policy story says monitoring detected errors and an engineer was paged a few minutes later.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Soon afterward:
claims.rationale: The timing is unsupported narrative precision.

claims.claim_text: Cilium graduated from CNCF on October 11, 2023 and CNCF graduation reflects project maturity and due diligence.
claims.rationale: The CNCF announcement directly states the graduation date and describes the due diligence, audit, and maturity validation.

claims.claim_text: Cilium was the first graduated project in the cloud native networking category.
claims.anchor_text: becoming the first graduated project in the cloud native networking category
claims.suggested_rewrite: becoming a graduated cloud native networking project
claims.rationale: The CNCF announcement supports graduation but does not substantiate the first-in-category wording.

claims.claim_text: Linux 5.6 was released in March 2020.
claims.rationale: The Linux v5.6 GitHub release tag shows the Linux 5.6 release date.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard was included in the Linux 5.6 kernel source tree.
claims.rationale: The Linux v5.6 source tree contains the drivers/net/wireguard implementation.

claims.claim_text: WireGuard in Linux is roughly a few thousand lines of audited code.
claims.anchor_text: just kernel-native crypto running in roughly a few thousand lines of audited code.
claims.suggested_rewrite: kernel-native encryption handled below the application layer.
claims.rationale: The code-size and audit claim is unsourced and more specific than the allowlisted kernel references support.

claims.claim_text: Cilium-generated dataplane bytecode is structured to stay well under the eBPF verifier ceiling across kernel versions.
claims.anchor_text: Cilium's compiler is structured to keep generated bytecode well under this ceiling so the dataplane stays loadable across kernel versions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cilium's generated dataplane programs still have to stay within verifier limits to load successfully across kernel versions.
claims.rationale: The specific claim about Cilium compiler structure is not directly supported by the kernel verifier source.

claims.claim_text: Hubble was announced and open-sourced by Isovalent in November 2019.
claims.rationale: The primary announcement appears to be the Cilium blog, but cilium.io is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Hubble does not sample, and every flow Cilium handles is observable.
claims.anchor_text: Because flow capture happens in-kernel, Hubble does not sample — every flow Cilium handles is observable
claims.suggested_rewrite: Because Hubble consumes Cilium flow events, it can expose detailed flow and drop information without application instrumentation
claims.rationale: The no-sampling/every-flow wording is stronger than the Hubble docs support and ignores bounded buffer behavior.

further_reading.title: Cilium Repository README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream overview of Cilium's eBPF dataplane, identity-aware policy, kube-proxy replacement, and observability features.

further_reading.title: Hubble Repository README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream overview of Hubble flow visibility, metrics, service maps, and policy troubleshooting workflows.

further_reading.title: NFTables mode for kube-proxy
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains kube-proxy iptables scaling limits and the Service proxying performance model in large clusters.

further_reading.title: Linux eBPF Verifier Documentation
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary kernel documentation for verifier safety checks and rejection behavior.

notes: No shared citation pool exists for platform-toolkits-infrastructure-networking-networking. Quiz answers, hands-on exercise steps, and code blocks were excluded unless the surrounding prose made an unsupported factual claim.
